content.text: Production agentic systems operate across networks of LLM APIs, external tools, databases, and sandboxed code runners — all of which fail intermittently. A robust agent does not simply crash on the first timeout or rate-limit response; instead it classifies each failure, decides whether to retry, fall back to an alternative, or surface a structured error, and resumes from a known checkpoint when possible. Getting this right separates reliable agents from fragile demos.
What it is
Retry

is the automatic re-execution of a failed operation after a waiting period, up to a configured attempt limit. The waiting strategy — how long to wait and whether that wait grows over successive attempts — is called a backoff policy. The simplest policy is a fixed delay; the most common production policy is
exponential backoff with jitter
, where the delay roughly doubles each attempt and a random offset prevents synchronized thundering herds of retrying workers.
Error recovery

is the broader discipline of deciding what to do once retries are exhausted or inapplicable. Recovery options include: switching to a fallback model or tool, returning a cached or degraded result, escalating to a human, saving the agent's state as a checkpoint so the run can resume later, or raising a structured fatal error that a calling orchestrator can handle. Together, retry and recovery define an agent's
resilience contract
— the explicit policy the system follows under failure.
Retries and recovery are distinct from error
detection

. Detection (checking status codes, parsing API error bodies, catching exceptions) is a prerequisite; retry and recovery are the policy layer that acts on what detection finds. Both layers must be explicit and observable — silent retries that swallow errors make debugging nearly impossible.
Why it matters

Every dependency an agent touches can fail. LLM provider APIs enforce per-minute and per-day rate limits and occasionally return HTTP 500 responses during platform incidents. Vector databases time out under heavy concurrent load. Code execution sandboxes run out of memory on large inputs. Third-party REST APIs return 503 during their own deployments. In a long multi-step agent run — say, 30 tool calls over five minutes — even a 2% per-call failure rate means nearly half of all runs hit at least one transient error.

Without retry logic, every transient blip aborts the entire task and the user gets a raw error message. Without a recovery policy, agents either silently swallow errors (hallucinating success) or propagate raw exceptions into the conversation in a way the model cannot reason about. Explicit retry-and-recovery design converts unpredictable infrastructure noise into predictable, observable, bounded behavior.
At scale, retry strategy also becomes a
cost control

lever. An agent that retries an LLM call up to five times with exponential backoff spends far less over time than one that immediately re-queues the full task and restarts from scratch. Checkpoint-based recovery makes long tasks economically viable by eliminating the need to re-do completed steps.
Benefits
Higher task completion rate
— transient failures (rate limits, short network outages, temporary capacity issues) are absorbed automatically without user intervention.
Graceful degradation
— when the primary path is unavailable, fallbacks (a smaller model, a cached retrieval result, a simplified tool set) keep the agent producing useful output.
Cost efficiency
— capped retries and backoff reduce redundant API calls; checkpointing avoids re-running completed steps after a fatal failure.
Observability
— explicit retry counters and error-class metrics surface systemic instability that would otherwise be invisible (e.g., a tool that fails silently 30% of the time).
Predictable latency SLOs
— bounded retry budgets (max attempts + wall-clock timeout) make it possible to reason about worst-case agent run time.
Clean failure modes
— structured error returns (not raw exceptions) let the model or orchestrator decide what to do next rather than crashing the process.
Trade-offs
Added latency

— each retry cycle adds wall-clock time. With five attempts and exponential backoff (base 1 s, cap 60 s), the worst-case wait is 1 + 2 + 4 + 8 + 16 = 31 s before the final attempt even starts. Users and orchestrators need progress signals.
Duplicate side effects
— retrying a non-idempotent operation (a payment, a send-email call, a database insert without a unique key) may execute it twice. Idempotency must be handled at the tool layer, not assumed.
Masking persistent bugs

— a misconfigured API key returns 401 forever; an agent that retries indefinitely wastes budget and time without ever succeeding. Error classification is essential: only retry errors that are genuinely transient.
Cascading load
— in multi-agent systems, a wave of simultaneous retries against a struggling service makes the problem worse (thundering herd). Jitter and per-tenant retry budgets are required at scale.
Checkpoint storage overhead
— saving state between turns adds storage writes and serialization costs. The checkpoint must be consistent (not mid-operation).
How it works
Exponential backoff formula
The canonical backoff delay before attempt
(zero-indexed) is:
where
is the initial delay (typically 1 second),
is the maximum delay (commonly 30–60 seconds), and
is a uniform random jitter multiplier. The jitter spreads retry attempts across time so that many agents whose requests failed together do not all retry at the same instant.
Error classification
Before deciding whether to retry, the agent must classify the error. The three standard classes are:
Transient
— the operation may succeed if retried immediately after a short wait. Examples: HTTP 429 (rate limit), HTTP 503 (service unavailable), network timeout, connection reset. These are the
only
errors that should trigger a retry loop.
Recoverable

— retrying the same operation will not help, but an alternative approach is available. Examples: the primary model is over capacity (fall back to a smaller model), the vector store is down (use keyword search), a tool returns malformed output (ask the model to replan without that tool).
Fatal

— neither retrying nor falling back will help. Examples: HTTP 401 (wrong credentials), HTTP 400 with a schema validation error, budget exceeded, context window overflowed. These should raise immediately, checkpoint state, and escalate.
Retry budget
A retry budget consists of two independent limits: a
maximum attempt count
(e.g. 5) and a
wall-clock deadline

(e.g. 120 seconds from the first attempt). The operation fails as soon as either limit is reached. Using only an attempt count is insufficient — with very long backoff delays, five attempts can still consume many minutes.
Tool idempotency

Before retrying a tool call, the agent must know whether the tool is idempotent. A tool is idempotent if calling it twice with the same arguments produces the same state as calling it once — GET requests, read operations, and writes that use idempotency keys qualify. Non-idempotent tools (send email, charge payment, append to a log) must either use an idempotency key supplied by the agent, or must not be retried without explicit deduplication at the server side.
Checkpointing

For long agent runs, checkpointing saves the accumulated conversation state (messages, tool results, intermediate outputs) to durable storage after each successful turn. On a fatal error the run aborts, but can be resumed from the last checkpoint rather than restarted from scratch. Checkpoints must be written atomically — a partial write is worse than no checkpoint because it can corrupt state.
Architecture

The diagram below shows the full decision flow an agent loop follows on each tool or API invocation — from execution to success, and through the three error classes to their respective handling paths.

attrs.alt: Retry and error recovery decision flow for agent loops

content.text: The key insight is that error handling is a
policy layer

that wraps every tool call. The agent loop itself does not contain retry logic inline; instead a thin decorator or middleware intercepts errors and applies the correct policy, then either returns a result or raises a structured error that the loop handles. This separation keeps the loop logic clean and makes the retry policy independently testable.
When to use it
Retry transient errors for:
LLM completion calls — rate limits (429) and transient 5xx responses
Vector store and embedding API calls — network timeouts, capacity spikes
Third-party REST GET endpoints — short outages, CDN hiccups
Code execution sandbox startup — occasional cold-start failures
Do
not
blindly retry:
4xx client errors other than 429 — these indicate a bug in the request; fix the code, not the retry count.
Non-idempotent tool calls (payment, send-email, database insert) unless an idempotency key is included in every call.
Calls that have already consumed significant context (large summarizations) — duplicate LLM calls multiply token costs with no benefit if the root cause is permanent.
Use fallback recovery

when: the primary LLM model is unavailable but a smaller model can satisfy the request at lower quality; a real-time retrieval tool is down but a cached or keyword-search result is acceptable; a specialized tool returns an error but the model can replan around the missing capability.
Use checkpointing

whenever: individual turns cost more than a few seconds of compute; the user's task spans multiple logical phases; the system is running in an environment with unreliable infrastructure (cloud spot instances, preemptible VMs).
How to implement
The recommended pattern is a
retry decorator
that wraps individual tool or API functions, paired with an error-aware agent loop that handles structured error returns from exhausted retries.
Step-by-step implementation:
Define error classes
— subclass your base exception into Transient, Recoverable, and Fatal so the retry layer can pattern-match without inspecting HTTP status codes inline.
Write a backoff utility
— a single function that computes the delay for attempt
n
using the exponential formula with jitter and a cap.
Wrap each external call
— apply the retry decorator to LLM completion, tool dispatch, and any network I/O. Keep the decorator thin; it should not contain business logic.
Return structured errors from tools
— when a tool call fails permanently (after retries), return a
dict rather than raising into the loop. This lets the model decide whether to replan.
Add a global timeout
— wrap the entire agent run in a deadline so retries cannot extend runtime indefinitely.
Checkpoint after each turn
— serialize messages + tool results to durable storage before returning control to the caller.
Examples
Example 1 — simple retry with tenacity
The
tenacity
library is the most widely used Python retry library. It composes retry conditions, backoff strategies, and stop conditions declaratively and integrates cleanly with async code.

# pip install tenacity anthropic
from tenacity import (
    retry,
    stop_after_attempt,
    wait_exponential_jitter,
    retry_if_exception_type,
    before_sleep_log,
)
import logging
import anthropic

log = logging.getLogger(__name__)
client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# Only retry rate-limit and server errors; fail fast on 4xx client errors.
RETRYABLE = (anthropic.RateLimitError, anthropic.InternalServerError)

@retry(
    retry=retry_if_exception_type(RETRYABLE),
    wait=wait_exponential_jitter(initial=1, max=30, jitter=2),
    stop=stop_after_attempt(5),
    before_sleep=before_sleep_log(log, logging.WARNING),
    reraise=True,
)
def call_claude(messages: list[dict]) -> str:
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=1024,
        messages=messages,
    )
    return response.content[0].text


if __name__ == "__main__":
    result = call_claude([{"role": "user", "content": "Summarize exponential backoff in one sentence."}])
    print(result)
Example 2 — agent loop with error classification and fallback

This example implements a complete resilient agent turn: the LLM call is retried on transient errors; tool failures are returned as structured error messages so the model can replan; a fallback model is tried if the primary is unavailable; and state is checkpointed before each yield.
Real-world example

A document-processing pipeline ingests customer contracts (100–500 pages each) and uses an agent to extract structured data: parties, dates, clauses, and financial terms. Each contract takes 40–90 API calls (chunked extraction, summarization, validation). The system runs on spot instances and must handle premature termination gracefully.
The production architecture uses three layers of resilience:
Per-call retry
— each LLM completion and embedding call is wrapped with a tenacity decorator (max 5 attempts, exponential backoff, respects Retry-After header).
Per-document checkpointing
— after every 10 chunks are processed, the agent serializes {chunk_index, extracted_fields, messages} to an S3 key. On restart the agent loads the checkpoint and skips completed chunks.
Dead-letter queue
— contracts that fail all retries are enqueued to an SQS dead-letter queue with the last error payload. A human reviewer inspects them and either fixes the input or flags the document as unsupported.

In six months of production operation, this pipeline achieves a 99.2% contract completion rate; the remaining 0.8% route to the dead-letter queue for human triage. Without checkpointing, spot-instance preemptions alone caused an 18% re-run rate.
Common mistakes
Retrying all exception types
Wrapping every exception in a retry loop means permanent errors (wrong API key, malformed request, budget exceeded) are retried until the attempt limit is hit, wasting time and money.
No jitter on backoff
Fixed exponential backoff without jitter causes a
thundering herd
when many agents fail at the same time (e.g., after a provider incident). All agents compute the same delay and retry simultaneously, overloading the recovering service.

# WRONG — synchronized retries
delay = base * (2 ** attempt)

# RIGHT — desynchronized retries
delay = min(cap, base * (2 ** attempt)) * random.uniform(0.5, 1.5)
Retrying non-idempotent tools

Retrying a charge-payment or send-email tool call without an idempotency key results in double charges or duplicate messages. The retry layer cannot know whether the first call succeeded before the network dropped the response.
Nested retry layers
If both the agent's tool wrapper and the underlying HTTP client have independent retry policies, a single logical failure can trigger up to

actual HTTP calls. Always configure retry at exactly one level — prefer the application layer so business logic (error classification, idempotency) can be applied, and disable automatic retries in the HTTP client library.
Best practices
Set both a max-attempt count and a wall-clock deadline
— use whichever limit is hit first to bound worst-case run time.
Classify errors before retrying
— maintain explicit lists of transient, recoverable, and fatal exception types; update them when you add new tools.
Honor Retry-After headers
— rate-limit responses from well-behaved APIs include a Retry-After value; wait at least that long rather than the computed backoff delay.
Use idempotency keys for all write operations
— generate a UUID per logical operation, not per retry attempt, and pass it in every call.
Return structured errors from tools
— a dict like
{"error": "rate limit", "retryable": true}
lets the model replan intelligently rather than propagating a Python stack trace.
Keep retry logic at one layer
— disable HTTP-client-level retries when the application layer handles them, to avoid multiplicative retry storms.
Emit retry metrics
— log attempt count, error class, and final outcome for every retried call; alert when the retry rate for any tool exceeds a threshold.
Checkpoint atomically
— write checkpoint data transactionally or use a staging key and rename to avoid reading a partial checkpoint after a crash.
Performance & scalability

Backoff with jitter is inherently self-throttling, but at scale additional controls are needed. In a system with thousands of concurrent agents, the aggregate retry traffic from a single provider incident can be a multiple of normal load — enough to prevent the provider from recovering. Circuit breakers address this at the service level.
A
circuit breaker
tracks the failure rate of a dependency over a rolling window. When the rate exceeds a threshold (e.g., 50% failure over the last 60 seconds) the breaker trips to
open
state and returns errors immediately without attempting the call. After a cooling-off period it moves to
half-open
and allows a single probe request; on success it resets to closed.

For per-tenant fairness, enforce separate retry budgets per user or organization. A single heavy user retrying aggressively should not consume the retry capacity of other tenants. This is typically implemented as a rate-limiting token bucket per tenant ID applied before the retry loop, not inside it.

Checkpoint I/O can become a bottleneck at high throughput. Mitigations include: writing checkpoints asynchronously (fire-and-forget with a flush on shutdown), compressing the serialized state, and checkpointing every N turns rather than every turn. The trade-off is that async checkpoints may be one turn behind if the process crashes between the write and the ack.
Tooling & ecosystem
tenacity (Python)
The de-facto standard Python retry library. Provides composable stop, wait, and retry conditions; sync and async support; structured logging of retry events; and statistics collection. Use
wait_exponential_jitter
for LLM API calls.
stamina (Python — newer alternative)
stamina

is a newer, opinionated retry library built on top of tenacity that makes sensible defaults (exponential backoff with jitter, structured logging) the path of least resistance. It also integrates with OpenTelemetry for distributed tracing.
pybreaker (Python circuit breaker)

Implements the circuit-breaker pattern with configurable failure thresholds, reset timeouts, and thread-safe state management. Pairs well with tenacity: the circuit breaker wraps the outer call, while tenacity decorates the inner HTTP attempt.
LangGraph / LangChain
LangGraph's node execution model integrates checkpointing via
SqliteSaver
or
PostgresSaver
— every graph node persists state after execution. Retry can be added at the LLM call level using LangChain's
BaseRetryOutputParser
or by wrapping the LLM class with a tenacity decorator.
AWS Step Functions / Google Workflows

Managed orchestration services implement retry and error-handling at the workflow level, separate from application code. Step Functions supports per-state retry policies with backoff, jitter, and error-type filtering. These are useful when the agent runs as a series of Lambda functions or containerized tasks, allowing infra-level durability without application-level checkpointing.
Comparison with alternatives
Several resilience patterns can substitute or complement retries. The right choice depends on the failure mode, operation semantics, and latency requirements.
Pattern
What it does
Best for
Limitation
Exponential backoff + retry
Re-executes the same call after a growing delay
Transient errors on idempotent operations
Adds latency; unsafe for non-idempotent calls
Circuit breaker
Stops calling a failing dependency until it recovers
High-volume systems; preventing cascading failure
Adds open/half-open complexity; needs tuning
Fallback
Switches to an alternate implementation on failure
Graceful degradation; availability > quality
Fallback may be lower quality or slower
Dead-letter queue
Defers failed tasks to a queue for later processing or inspection
Async pipelines; human triage of hard failures
Introduces async complexity; latency for the failed request
Saga / compensating transaction
Undoes completed steps when a later step fails
Multi-step writes that must be atomic
Complex to design; requires compensating logic for each step
Bulkhead
Isolates failures by limiting concurrency per dependency
Preventing one slow dependency from blocking all resources
Does not improve success rate; only limits blast radius
Security & reliability
Retry amplification attacks

If an attacker can trigger agent runs that produce high retry rates (e.g., by supplying inputs that cause tool calls to always return 429), the agent may exhaust its API quota rapidly. Mitigate by: enforcing a per-user retry budget, rate-limiting run submissions per user, and alerting on abnormally high retry counts for a single run.
Credential exposure in retried requests

If a retry logs the full request body for debugging (common in development) and the body contains user data or credentials, those are written to logs on every retry. Ensure log sanitization removes sensitive fields before recording retry payloads.
Idempotency key replay attacks
Idempotency keys must be scoped to the authenticated user and operation. A key generated from predictable inputs (e.g.,
hash(user_id + amount)
) can be guessed by an attacker to replay a previously successful payment. Always generate keys from cryptographically random sources (e.g.,
uuid.uuid4()
Checkpoint integrity

Checkpoints contain the agent's full conversation state, potentially including confidential user data. Encrypt checkpoint data at rest (S3 SSE, database column-level encryption) and in transit. Apply access controls so only the agent process and authorized operators can read checkpoint files. Verify checkpoint integrity on load using a HMAC or content hash to detect corruption.
Blast radius bounding

Any individual agent run should have a hard cost cap (maximum tokens, maximum wall-clock time, maximum spend in USD). Retries can linearly amplify token consumption — a run that hits its retry limit on every LLM call may spend 5x the expected tokens. Instrument token usage per run and abort when the cap is hit, saving a checkpoint before raising the budget error.
Key takeaways
Classify every error as transient, recoverable, or fatal before deciding whether to retry — only transient errors should trigger a retry loop.
Use exponential backoff with full jitter and a cap to limit wait time and prevent thundering-herd effects in multi-agent systems.
Always set both a max-attempt count and a wall-clock deadline; either limit should abort the retry loop.
Non-idempotent tool calls (payments, emails, database writes) must use idempotency keys before they can be safely retried.
Return structured error dicts from tools rather than propagating raw exceptions — this lets the model replan without crashing the agent loop.
Checkpoint agent state to durable storage after each successful turn so long-running tasks can resume from a known-good state instead of restarting.
Apply circuit breakers in high-throughput systems to stop calling a failing dependency entirely and give it time to recover.
Keep retry logic at exactly one layer; nested retries at the application and HTTP-client layers multiply call counts unexpectedly.
Emit retry-count and error-class metrics for every tool; high retry rates on a specific tool signal systemic instability worth fixing at the root.
